title: Slack webhook URL accidentally pasted into workflow JSON
symptom: A Slack webhook URL appears in workflow configuration, exported JSON, or version control. This exposes the ability for anyone to post messages to the Slack channel.
applies_when: Workflow JSON was exported or committed to git
Slack Incoming Webhook URL is hardcoded in a node configuration
not_this_if: URL is stored in credentials/environment variables
root_cause: Slack Incoming Webhook URLs are secret — anyone with the URL can post messages to the channel. When users paste the URL directly into n8n node fields, it gets embedded in the workflow JSON and can leak via git commits, exports, or logs.
fix_steps: Remove the webhook URL from the workflow JSON immediately
Rotate the Slack webhook URL in Slack admin > Apps > Incoming Webhooks
Store the URL in n8n credentials or environment variables instead
Check git history for the leaked URL and consider it compromised
agent_instruction: The Slack webhook URL is exposed in the workflow. Move it to n8n credentials or environment variables. Rotate the compromised URL in Slack admin. Check git history for the leak.
safety_notes: Treat webhook URLs as secrets — they grant write access to Slack channels
A leaked URL must be rotated, not just removed from current files
